content.problemStatement:
Life is change, change is stability. \textit {Life is change, change is stability.}Life is change, change is stability.

Chef calls a sequence of integers A1,A2,…,ANA_1, A_2, \ldots, A_NA1​,A2​,…,AN​ `stable` if all the elements in the sequence are equal.

Chef gives you a sequence A1,A2,…,ANA_1, A_2, \ldots, A_NA1​,A2​,…,AN​. You may perform the following operations on the sequence any number of times (possibly 000):



Select any prefix A[1…k]A[1 \ldots k]A[1…k] such that max(A[1…k])=A[k]max(A[1 \ldots k]) = A[k]max(A[1…k])=A[k] and set A[i]:=A[k]A[i] := A[k]A[i]:=A[k] ∀i∈[1,k−1]\forall i \in [1,k-1]∀i∈[1,k−1]. For example, if the sequence A=[3,1,4,5,9]A = [\textcolor{blue}{3,1,4},5,9]A=[3,1,4,5,9], then choosing k=3k = 3k=3 yields the sequence [4,4,4,5,9][\textcolor{blue}{4,4,4},5,9][4,4,4,5,9].




Select any segment A[L…R]A[L \ldots R]A[L…R] and shift the segment cyclically to the left keeping other elements unchanged. For example, if the sequence A=[3,4,1,5,9]A = [3, \textcolor{blue}{4,1,5} ,9]A=[3,4,1,5,9], then choosing L=2L = 2L=2 and R=4R = 4R=4 yields the sequence [3,1,5,4,9][3, \textcolor{blue}{1,5,4} ,9][3,1,5,4,9].



Chef is asking you to find the minimum number of operations required to make the sequence `stable`.

It is guaranteed that you can always make the sequence `stable` using such operations.


Input Format

The first line of the input contains a single integer TTT denoting the number of test cases. The description of TTT test cases follows.
The first line of each test case contains a single integer NNN.
The second line of each test case contains NNN space-separated integers A1,A2,…,ANA_1, A_2, \ldots, A_NA1​,A2​,…,AN​.


Output Format
For each test case, print the minimum number of operations required to make the sequence `stable`.
content.inputFormat:
The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Then for each test case:
- A line with an integer N, the length of the sequence.
- A line with N space‑separated integers A1 … AN.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single integer – the minimum number of operations required to make the whole sequence stable.

sampleTestCases.explanation: First test case: all elements are already equal → 0 operations. Second test case: the last element (96) is the global maximum, so one prefix operation makes the whole array [96,96].

Case 1: maximum (5) is at position 1, not at the end → shift segment [1,4] left (array becomes [2,3,1,5]) then one prefix operation → total 2. Case 2: last element is already the maximum (5) → one prefix operation. Case 3: already stable → 0.

hidden.label: maximum boundary (15 elements, max not at end)
edge case – all equal but max at end
general case 1 – max already at end
general case 2 – max not at end

solution.approach:
The only way to change many elements at once is the prefix operation. It can turn the whole array into the global maximum in a single step, but it requires that the maximum element be at the last position of the considered prefix. Therefore:
1. If all elements are already equal, no operation is needed.
2. If the last element A[N] is already the global maximum of the array, we can apply the prefix operation on the whole array once and obtain a stable sequence – answer = 1.
3. Otherwise we first bring a maximum element to the end. Using the cyclic‑left‑shift operation on the segment [pos, N] (where pos is any position of a maximum) moves that maximum to position N in exactly one operation. After that we apply the prefix operation – total answer = 2.
Thus the answer is:
- 0 if all elements are equal;
- 1 if A[N] equals the maximum value of the array;
- 2 otherwise.
The algorithm runs in O(N) per test case.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N) per test case, O(T·N) overall.
